content.verdict: Drupal Commerce is an e-commerce module bolted onto the Drupal CMS, so it fits stores that need commerce woven into content-heavy pages using Drupal's media library, layout builder, and Search API. Solidus is a standalone Ruby on Rails commerce framework with a native subscriptions engine and both REST and GraphQL APIs, built for developers who want a dedicated commerce codebase rather than a CMS add-on. Both are free and open source at the core, but they assume different teams: PHP/Drupal shops versus Ruby on Rails shops.
content.bestForToolA: Organizations already running Drupal (or wanting deep content management via Drupal's media library, layout builder, and Search API) that want commerce added to an existing content-driven site, maintained by Centarro since 2010.
content.bestForToolB: Ruby on Rails teams building a dedicated commerce application who want a native subscriptions engine and a choice between REST and GraphQL APIs, or who want the option to move to the fully hosted Solidus Cloud (Enterprise) tier later.
content.whoNeedsBoth: There isn't a realistic scenario for running both together long-term — they're alternative foundations for the same job (Drupal-based versus Rails-based commerce). The closest overlap is a team migrating a Drupal Commerce site to Solidus (or vice versa) running both temporarily during a rebuild.

keyDifferences.title: Underlying Platform
keyDifferences.toolA: Drupal Commerce is a module that runs on top of the Drupal CMS, inheriting Drupal's media library, layout builder, and Search API for product pages.
keyDifferences.toolB: Solidus is a standalone Ruby on Rails framework forked from Spree in 2015; it has no bundled CMS layer of its own.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: The choice determines whether commerce is layered onto an existing content platform or built as a dedicated Rails application from scratch.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Content-heavy publishers and multi-site organizations already invested in Drupal benefit from Drupal Commerce; greenfield Rails teams benefit from Solidus.

keyDifferences.title: Native Subscriptions
keyDifferences.toolA: Drupal Commerce's documented features cover multi-site/multi-currency support, promotions, and coupon management, but a native subscriptions engine is not documented.
keyDifferences.toolB: Solidus ships a native subscriptions engine designed for curated boxes and recurring purchase models.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: Recurring-revenue merchants need subscription billing logic built in rather than bolted on through third-party modules.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Subscription box and recurring-purchase businesses evaluating Solidus's built-in engine.

keyDifferences.title: API Architecture
keyDifferences.toolA: Drupal Commerce integrates with third-party applications via APIs and Drupal's event system, but a dedicated GraphQL API is not documented.
keyDifferences.toolB: Solidus offers both REST and GraphQL APIs for building custom frontends and integrations.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: Teams building headless or decoupled storefronts often need GraphQL specifically for efficient, flexible data fetching.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Developers building custom or headless storefronts who want a documented GraphQL option in Solidus.

keyDifferences.title: Managed Hosting Path
keyDifferences.toolA: Drupal Commerce's paid option is consulting and development services through maintainer Centarro, not a packaged hosted product.
keyDifferences.toolB: Solidus offers Solidus Cloud (Enterprise) as a separate, fully hosted plan with 24/7 support and unlimited usage, though its price is not publicly listed.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: Teams that eventually want to offload hosting and support need to know whether a packaged managed tier exists.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Growing merchants who may want to hand off infrastructure and support to Solidus Cloud rather than self-host indefinitely.

keyDifferences.title: Content & Search Tooling
keyDifferences.toolA: Drupal Commerce integrates with Drupal's Search API for keyword search and faceted filtering, and lets merchants configure custom fields and layouts through Drupal's field and layout systems.
keyDifferences.toolB: Solidus documents merchandising and personalization using first-party data, upsells, and content integration, but not a dedicated faceted search API.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: Stores with large, content-rich catalogs need robust search and layout tooling out of the box.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Catalog-heavy merchants and content teams who rely on Drupal's Search API and layout builder for product discovery.

rows.note: Drupal Commerce runs inside Drupal; Solidus is commerce-only
Solidus supports custom promotion rules beyond simple discount codes
Solidus documents internationalization as a distinct feature
Solidus Cloud (Enterprise); price not published
Drupal Commerce via Centarro; Solidus Cloud includes 24/7 support
Commerce Core 3.2.0 released September 2025

faqs.question: Is Drupal Commerce free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Drupal Commerce is free and open source; paid consulting and development services are available separately through maintainer Centarro.

faqs.question: Is Solidus free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Solidus is a free, open-source framework that can be self-hosted; a separate paid Solidus Cloud (Enterprise) tier exists for fully hosted deployments.

faqs.question: Does Solidus support subscription commerce?
faqs.answer: Yes, Solidus includes a native subscriptions engine for curated boxes and recurring purchase models, which is not documented as a Drupal Commerce feature.

faqs.question: Do I need Drupal to use Drupal Commerce?
faqs.answer: Yes, Drupal Commerce is a module that runs on top of the Drupal content management system, unlike Solidus, which is a standalone Ruby on Rails framework.

faqs.question: Which one offers a fully managed hosting option?
faqs.answer: Solidus offers Solidus Cloud (Enterprise) as a fully hosted plan with 24/7 support; Drupal Commerce's paid offering is consulting through Centarro rather than a packaged hosting product.

faqs.question: What programming stack does each require?
faqs.answer: Drupal Commerce requires PHP and Drupal CMS knowledge, while Solidus requires Ruby on Rails development expertise.

content.verdict: Drupal Commerce is a commerce module built into the Drupal CMS, best suited to content-driven sites that need product selling layered onto pages already using Drupal's media library and layout builder. GrandNode is a standalone ASP.NET Core platform with explicit multi-vendor marketplace, B2B, and booking/reservation features, aimed at merchants who need those specific business models without a CMS underneath. Both are free and open source, but they target different technology stacks (PHP/Drupal versus .NET) and different commerce shapes.
content.bestForToolA: Teams that already run or plan to run a Drupal site and want commerce integrated with Drupal's content tools, custom fields, and Search API rather than a separate platform.
content.bestForToolB: Merchants who specifically need B2B customer grouping and custom pricing, a multi-vendor marketplace, or booking/reservation-based products, built on the ASP.NET Core/.NET stack.
content.whoNeedsBoth: No typical scenario exists for running both simultaneously — they are competing foundations for the same storefront. The only overlap would be a business unit on Drupal Commerce spinning up a separate marketplace or booking site on GrandNode because Drupal Commerce doesn't document that functionality.

keyDifferences.title: Technology Stack
keyDifferences.toolA: Drupal Commerce is a PHP-based module running inside the Drupal CMS.
keyDifferences.toolB: GrandNode is built on ASP.NET Core / .NET and released under the GPL-3 license.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: The hosting stack determines which developers and infrastructure a team needs to run and maintain the store.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: PHP/Drupal teams benefit from Drupal Commerce; .NET teams benefit from GrandNode.

keyDifferences.title: Multi-Vendor Marketplace
keyDifferences.toolA: Drupal Commerce's documented features focus on multi-site, multi-language, multi-currency, and layout tooling; marketplace functionality is not documented.
keyDifferences.toolB: GrandNode includes explicit multi-vendor marketplace functionality for running multi-vendor storefronts.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: Marketplace models require vendor onboarding, split payouts, and vendor-scoped catalogs that a single-seller platform doesn't need to support.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Businesses building a multi-vendor marketplace rather than a single-seller storefront.

keyDifferences.title: B2B Store Management
keyDifferences.toolA: Drupal Commerce documents custom fields and layouts but no dedicated B2B customer grouping or tiered pricing feature.
keyDifferences.toolB: GrandNode explicitly supports B2C and B2B store management with customer grouping and custom pricing.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: B2B buyers often need negotiated pricing tiers and account-based access that consumer-only platforms don't model directly.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Wholesale and B2B sellers who need customer-group-specific pricing out of the box.

keyDifferences.title: Booking & Reservation Products
keyDifferences.toolA: Not documented as a Drupal Commerce feature.
keyDifferences.toolB: GrandNode supports booking/reservation-based products such as accommodations and services.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: Selling time-bound services (rentals, appointments, stays) requires dedicated inventory logic that standard product catalogs don't provide.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Businesses selling bookable services or accommodations alongside physical goods.

keyDifferences.title: Cross-Channel Integration
keyDifferences.toolA: Documents general third-party API integrations via Drupal's event system, but no named marketplace channel integrations.
keyDifferences.toolB: GrandNode explicitly integrates with channels such as Amazon and eBay, including inventory sync.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: Sellers who also list on third-party marketplaces need inventory to stay synchronized automatically to avoid overselling.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Merchants who sell simultaneously on Amazon, eBay, and their own storefront.

rows.note: GrandNode documents a dedicated headless API architecture
Drupal Commerce via Centarro; GrandNode offers optional premium support with pricing not published

faqs.question: Is Drupal Commerce free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Drupal Commerce is free and open source, with paid consulting available separately through Centarro.

faqs.question: Is GrandNode free?
faqs.answer: Yes, GrandNode's core platform is free and open source under the GPL-3 license, with optional paid support packages.

faqs.question: Which platform supports multi-vendor marketplaces?
faqs.answer: GrandNode explicitly documents multi-vendor marketplace functionality; this is not documented for Drupal Commerce.

faqs.question: Does either platform handle B2B pricing?
faqs.answer: GrandNode documents B2C and B2B store management with customer grouping and custom pricing; this is not documented as a Drupal Commerce feature.

faqs.question: What technology stack does each require?
faqs.answer: Drupal Commerce requires PHP and Drupal CMS knowledge, while GrandNode requires ASP.NET Core / .NET development expertise.

faqs.question: Can either sync inventory with Amazon or eBay?
faqs.answer: GrandNode documents cross-channel integration with Amazon and eBay including inventory sync; this is not documented for Drupal Commerce.

content.verdict: Both are free, open-source ASP.NET Core platforms, but they emphasize different things: GrandNode documents explicit B2B, multi-vendor marketplace, booking/reservation, and headless API capabilities, while nopCommerce leans on a long track record (first released in 2008, 3M+ downloads), a large plugin and theme marketplace, and built-in SEO tooling. Neither publishes a formal pricing plan array, since both are free-to-use software with optional paid support layered on top.
content.bestForToolA: Merchants who need built-in B2B customer grouping and pricing, a multi-vendor marketplace, or bookable/reservation products, and want a headless API architecture for custom frontends.
content.bestForToolB: Teams that want a mature, widely deployed platform (downloaded over 3 million times since 2008) with an extensive plugin and theme marketplace and built-in SEO-friendly URL and metadata tooling.
content.whoNeedsBoth: There's no realistic joint-use scenario — both are full storefront platforms competing for the same job on the same tech stack, so a merchant picks one rather than running both.

keyDifferences.title: Licensing Model
keyDifferences.toolA: GrandNode is released under the standard GPL-3 open-source license.
keyDifferences.toolB: nopCommerce is distributed under a custom nopCommerce Public License rather than a standard OSI-approved license, per its documented cons.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: Legal and procurement teams often require an OSI-approved license for open-source software adoption.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Organizations with strict open-source license compliance requirements benefit from clarity on GrandNode's GPL-3 status versus nopCommerce's custom license.

keyDifferences.title: Business Model Breadth
keyDifferences.toolA: GrandNode explicitly documents B2B store management, multi-vendor marketplace, booking/reservation products, and e-learning/digital product sales.
keyDifferences.toolB: nopCommerce's documented features center on multi-store support, a plugin/theme marketplace, SEO tools, a discount/promotion engine, and multi-currency/multi-language support, without documented B2B, marketplace, or booking features.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: Merchants with non-standard business models (marketplaces, bookings, B2B) need those capabilities built in rather than custom-built.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Marketplace operators, B2B sellers, and booking-based businesses evaluating GrandNode's documented feature set.

keyDifferences.title: Plugin and Theme Ecosystem
keyDifferences.toolA: Not documented as having a dedicated plugin/theme marketplace; GrandNode does offer optional premium support packages.
keyDifferences.toolB: nopCommerce has an extensive plugin and theme marketplace, though many of the add-ons in it are paid.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: A large add-on ecosystem can shortcut custom development for common storefront needs.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Merchants who prefer buying pre-built plugins and themes over custom .NET development.

keyDifferences.title: Built-In SEO Tooling
keyDifferences.toolA: Not documented as a distinct feature category for GrandNode.
keyDifferences.toolB: nopCommerce explicitly includes built-in SEO-friendly URL and metadata configuration tools.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: Organic search visibility depends on clean URLs and metadata control at the platform level.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: SEO-focused merchants who want URL and metadata controls built into the core platform.

keyDifferences.title: Track Record and Release Cadence
keyDifferences.toolA: Founding year and download figures are not documented in GrandNode's public facts.
keyDifferences.toolB: nopCommerce was first released in 2008, has been downloaded more than 3 million times, and had version 4.90.6 released in July 2026.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: A longer, well-documented track record signals platform maturity and an active maintenance cadence.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Risk-averse teams that weigh platform longevity and community size before committing.

rows.note: GrandNode uses GPL-3; nopCommerce uses a custom Public License
nopCommerce: 3M+ downloads since 2008

faqs.question: Are GrandNode and nopCommerce both free?
faqs.answer: Yes, both are free, open-source ASP.NET Core platforms with optional paid support available.

faqs.question: Which platform supports multi-vendor marketplaces or B2B pricing?
faqs.answer: GrandNode documents both multi-vendor marketplace functionality and B2B store management with customer grouping; these are not documented as nopCommerce features.

faqs.question: Which platform has a larger plugin ecosystem?
faqs.answer: nopCommerce documents an extensive plugin and theme marketplace, though many add-ons are paid; this is not documented for GrandNode.

faqs.question: Which platform is older and more widely deployed?
faqs.answer: nopCommerce was first released in 2008 and has been downloaded more than 3 million times, a track record that isn't documented for GrandNode.

faqs.question: Is either platform's license OSI-approved?
faqs.answer: GrandNode uses the standard GPL-3 license; nopCommerce uses a custom nopCommerce Public License rather than a standard OSI-approved license.

faqs.question: Does either support booking or reservation-based products?
faqs.answer: GrandNode documents support for booking/reservation products such as accommodations and services; this is not documented for nopCommerce.

content.verdict: nopCommerce is a full, free, self-hosted storefront platform with its own admin dashboard, plugin/theme marketplace, and SEO tooling, built for merchants who want to own the entire ASP.NET Core stack. Snipcart is a paid, usage-based drop-in cart and checkout snippet that layers commerce onto an existing site built in virtually any tech stack, charging 2% per transaction with a $20/month minimum. One is a platform you build a store on; the other is a checkout layer you add to a site you already have.
content.bestForToolA: Merchants who want a complete, self-hosted storefront with an admin dashboard, discount engine, and SEO-friendly URLs, and who have or can hire ASP.NET Core / .NET expertise.
content.bestForToolB: Developers who already have a site (Jamstack, React, Vue, WordPress, Laravel, or otherwise) and want to add a cart and checkout via an HTML/JS snippet without building or hosting a full commerce backend.
content.whoNeedsBoth: There's little practical overlap since both solve the storefront/checkout layer directly, but a team could prototype quickly with Snipcart's free testing environment on a marketing site before deciding whether to invest in a full nopCommerce build for a larger catalog.

keyDifferences.title: Pricing Structure
keyDifferences.toolA: nopCommerce is free to download, install, and use commercially with no license fee, distributed under the nopCommerce Public License.
keyDifferences.toolB: Snipcart charges 2% per transaction, with a $20/month minimum for stores generating under $1,000 USD in monthly sales.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: Transaction-based pricing scales with revenue and can add up for high-volume stores, while a free self-hosted platform shifts cost to infrastructure and development time instead.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: High-volume merchants may prefer nopCommerce's flat cost structure; low-volume or early-stage sellers may prefer Snipcart's pay-as-you-go model.

keyDifferences.title: Deployment Model
keyDifferences.toolA: nopCommerce is a full self-hosted platform including storefront, admin dashboard, and database (MS SQL Server backend).
keyDifferences.toolB: Snipcart is a drop-in cart snippet added to an existing site rather than a full storefront builder, per its own documented cons.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: Teams need to know whether they're building a whole site or bolting commerce onto one that already exists.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Teams starting from scratch benefit from nopCommerce's complete platform; teams with an existing site benefit from Snipcart's drop-in approach.

keyDifferences.title: Tech Stack Flexibility
keyDifferences.toolA: nopCommerce is built specifically on ASP.NET Core with an MS SQL Server backend.
keyDifferences.toolB: Snipcart is designed to work with virtually any tech stack, including Jamstack, React, Vue, WordPress, and Laravel sites.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: Teams not using .NET need a checkout solution that doesn't dictate their existing frontend or CMS choice.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Developers on non-.NET stacks (Jamstack, React, Vue, WordPress, Laravel) benefit from Snipcart's stack-agnostic integration.

keyDifferences.title: Abandoned Cart Recovery
keyDifferences.toolA: Not documented as a specific nopCommerce feature; nopCommerce instead documents a discount and promotion engine.
keyDifferences.toolB: Snipcart includes built-in tools to recover abandoned shopping carts.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: Recovering abandoned carts directly affects conversion rate and revenue without extra development work.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Merchants focused on conversion optimization who want abandoned-cart recovery out of the box in Snipcart.

keyDifferences.title: Free Testing / Trial Access
keyDifferences.toolA: Not documented as a formal trial program; the core platform itself is free to use commercially.
keyDifferences.toolB: Snipcart offers a free, unlimited testing environment with no credit card required before going live.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: A no-commitment sandbox lets developers validate integration before any transaction fees apply.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Developers evaluating Snipcart who want to test the full checkout flow risk-free before launch.

rows.note: Snipcart is a cart/checkout layer, not a full storefront builder
2% per transaction, $20/month minimum
No credit card required

rows.feature: Works with any frontend tech stack
rows.note: nopCommerce requires ASP.NET Core; Snipcart works with Jamstack, React, Vue, WordPress, Laravel

faqs.question: Does nopCommerce charge transaction fees?
faqs.answer: No, nopCommerce is free to download and use commercially with no license fee; Snipcart, by contrast, charges 2% per transaction with a $20/month minimum.

faqs.question: What's the minimum monthly cost for Snipcart?
faqs.answer: Snipcart's minimum fee is $20/month for stores generating under $1,000 USD in monthly sales; above that threshold, cost is purely the 2% transaction fee.

faqs.question: Can I add Snipcart to any website?
faqs.answer: Yes, Snipcart is documented to work with virtually any tech stack, including Jamstack, React, Vue, WordPress, and Laravel sites, via an HTML/JS snippet.

faqs.question: Does nopCommerce come with a full storefront and admin panel?
faqs.answer: Yes, nopCommerce is a complete self-hosted platform with an admin dashboard, discount engine, and plugin/theme marketplace, unlike Snipcart, which is a drop-in checkout layer.

faqs.question: Can Snipcart sell digital products?
faqs.answer: Yes, Snipcart supports selling both physical and digital products.

faqs.question: Which is better for a developer who already has a custom site?
faqs.answer: Snipcart is designed for exactly that case — adding a cart and checkout to an existing site without redirecting users elsewhere — while nopCommerce expects to be the site itself.

content.verdict: Grype and OpenVAS are both free, open-source vulnerability scanners, but they target different attack surfaces. Grype, from Anchore, scans container images, filesystems, and SBOMs for known vulnerabilities in OS packages and language dependencies, with EPSS/CISA KEV-based prioritization. OpenVAS, maintained by Greenbone since 2006, is a network vulnerability scanner that performs authenticated and unauthenticated testing across internet and industrial protocols, forming the core of Greenbone Community Edition. Neither replaces the other because they scan fundamentally different things.
content.bestForToolA: DevSecOps teams who need to scan container images, filesystems, or SBOM files (CycloneDX/SPDX) in CI/CD pipelines, with EPSS and CISA KEV data to prioritize which vulnerabilities to fix first.
content.bestForToolB: Security teams performing network vulnerability assessments against live infrastructure, including both authenticated and unauthenticated scans across internet and industrial protocols.
content.whoNeedsBoth: A realistic complementary setup: use Grype in CI/CD to catch dependency and container vulnerabilities before deployment, and run OpenVAS (or the broader Greenbone Community Edition it powers) against deployed infrastructure to catch network-facing exposures — supply-chain security and network security are different layers that both need coverage.

keyDifferences.title: Scan Target
keyDifferences.toolA: Grype scans container images, filesystems, and SBOM files for known vulnerabilities.
keyDifferences.toolB: OpenVAS performs authenticated and unauthenticated testing directly against network-facing systems across many protocol types.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: Supply-chain vulnerabilities in code dependencies and network-exposed service vulnerabilities require entirely different scanning techniques.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Application/DevSecOps teams need Grype's approach; infrastructure/network security teams need OpenVAS's approach.

keyDifferences.title: Ecosystem Coverage
keyDifferences.toolA: Grype covers OS packages from Alpine, Debian, Ubuntu, RHEL, Oracle Linux, and Amazon Linux, plus language dependencies in Ruby, Java, JavaScript, Python, .NET, Go, PHP, and Rust.
keyDifferences.toolB: OpenVAS is not documented as scanning language package dependencies; it focuses on internet and industrial protocol testing.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: Modern applications depend on many language ecosystems whose vulnerabilities live in dependency manifests, not network services.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Development teams tracking vulnerable dependencies across multiple programming languages benefit specifically from Grype's coverage.

keyDifferences.title: Vulnerability Prioritization
keyDifferences.toolA: Grype uses EPSS scores, CISA KEV data, and risk scoring to help prioritize which vulnerabilities to fix first, plus OpenVEX support to filter results.
keyDifferences.toolB: Not documented as an OpenVAS feature; OpenVAS pulls detection tests from a feed updated daily but doesn't document EPSS/KEV-based scoring.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: Prioritization data reduces alert fatigue by helping teams focus on vulnerabilities that are actually being exploited in the wild.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Teams drowning in vulnerability findings who need to triage by real-world exploitability benefit from Grype's EPSS/KEV integration.

keyDifferences.title: Feed Update Cadence
keyDifferences.toolA: Not documented with a specific update cadence claim.
keyDifferences.toolB: OpenVAS explicitly pulls vulnerability detection tests from a feed that is updated daily.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: Fresh detection signatures matter for catching newly disclosed network vulnerabilities quickly.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Network security teams who need documented, predictable feed freshness benefit from OpenVAS's daily update cadence.

keyDifferences.title: Deployment Form
keyDifferences.toolA: Grype is a command-line tool with no built-in graphical dashboard, installable via curl, Homebrew, Docker, Chocolatey, or MacPorts.
keyDifferences.toolB: OpenVAS is documented as best used as part of the broader Greenbone Community Edition rather than standalone, with enterprise features requiring Greenbone's commercial products.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: Teams need to know whether they're adopting a CLI utility for pipelines or a broader scanning suite for ongoing operations.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: CI/CD pipeline engineers benefit from Grype's lightweight CLI; security operations teams benefit from OpenVAS's role inside the fuller Greenbone suite.

rows.note: Ruby, Java, JavaScript, Python, .NET, Go, PHP, Rust
OpenVAS documented as best used within Greenbone Community Edition
Via Greenbone's commercial products

faqs.question: What does Grype scan that OpenVAS doesn't?
faqs.answer: Grype scans container images, filesystems, and SBOM files for OS package and language dependency vulnerabilities; this scan scope is not documented for OpenVAS.

faqs.question: What does OpenVAS scan that Grype doesn't?
faqs.answer: OpenVAS performs authenticated and unauthenticated network vulnerability scanning across internet and industrial protocols, which is not documented as a Grype capability.

faqs.question: Are both tools free?
faqs.answer: Yes, both Grype (Apache-2.0) and OpenVAS (core of Greenbone Community Edition) are free and open source.

faqs.question: Does Grype prioritize which vulnerabilities to fix first?
faqs.answer: Yes, Grype uses EPSS scores, CISA's Known Exploited Vulnerabilities list, and risk scoring to help prioritize remediation; this specific prioritization method is not documented for OpenVAS.

faqs.question: Can Grype and OpenVAS be used together?
faqs.answer: Yes — they address different layers, so many teams run Grype in CI/CD for container and dependency scanning while using OpenVAS for network-facing infrastructure scanning.

faqs.question: Does OpenVAS scan container images?
faqs.answer: This is not documented as an OpenVAS capability; its documented scope is network and protocol-based scanning rather than container image analysis.

content.verdict: Checkmarx One is a broad, AI-powered application security platform covering SAST, DAST, SCA, container, secrets, IaC, and API security, priced through custom enterprise quotes with no advertised free trial. OpenVAS is a free, open-source network vulnerability scanner maintained by Greenbone since 2006, focused on authenticated and unauthenticated protocol-level testing. They sit at different layers of a security program — application source code and pipelines versus network infrastructure — so choosing between them depends on what surface needs coverage.
content.bestForToolA: Enterprises needing a single AI-assisted platform to cover SAST, DAST, SCA, container, secrets, IaC, and API security across a large development organization, especially those needing SOC 2 Type II, ISO 27001, or FedRAMP-aligned vendors.
content.bestForToolB: Organizations that need free, self-hosted network vulnerability scanning with a daily-updated detection feed, particularly as part of the broader Greenbone Community Edition.
content.whoNeedsBoth: A realistic combination: an enterprise security program runs Checkmarx to scan application source code, dependencies, containers, and IaC templates across its development pipeline, while separately running OpenVAS (or Greenbone Community Edition) to perform network vulnerability scans of the servers and infrastructure that host those applications.

keyDifferences.title: Scan Coverage
keyDifferences.toolA: Checkmarx One covers NG SAST (source code analysis), DAST, SCA, container security, secrets detection, IaC security, and API security in one platform.
keyDifferences.toolB: OpenVAS performs authenticated and unauthenticated network vulnerability scanning across internet and industrial protocols; source code or dependency analysis is not documented.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: Application-layer vulnerabilities (in code, dependencies, containers) and network-layer vulnerabilities require fundamentally different scanning engines.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: AppSec teams responsible for the SDLC need Checkmarx's coverage; infrastructure/network security teams need OpenVAS's coverage.

keyDifferences.title: AI-Powered Remediation
keyDifferences.toolA: Checkmarx includes AI-powered agents — Developer Assist and Triage & Remediation Assist — plus a Checkmarx MCP Server to help developers fix findings faster.
keyDifferences.toolB: Not documented as an OpenVAS feature.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: AI-assisted triage and remediation can significantly reduce the manual effort of fixing large volumes of findings.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Development teams facing a high volume of security findings who want AI-assisted fixes benefit from Checkmarx's agents.

keyDifferences.title: Pricing Transparency
keyDifferences.toolA: Checkmarx pricing is fully custom, calculated on developers, apps, and usage, with no public price list and no advertised free trial.
keyDifferences.toolB: OpenVAS is free and open source, forming the core of the free Greenbone Community Edition, with no cost to start.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: Budget-constrained teams need to know upfront whether a tool is free to adopt or requires a sales conversation and enterprise budget.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Startups and small teams with limited security budgets benefit from OpenVAS's zero-cost entry point; large enterprises with dedicated AppSec budgets are the target for Checkmarx's custom pricing.

keyDifferences.title: Compliance Certifications
keyDifferences.toolA: Checkmarx holds SOC 2 Type II, ISO 27001 certification, and FedRAMP authorization, and was recognized as a Leader in the 2026 Gartner Magic Quadrant for Software Supply Chain Security.
keyDifferences.toolB: Not documented as holding formal compliance certifications; OpenVAS is instead positioned on its open-source pedigree and daily-updated feed since 2006.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: Regulated industries and government-adjacent buyers often require vendor compliance attestations before procurement.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Enterprises and government-adjacent buyers with formal vendor compliance requirements benefit from Checkmarx's documented certifications.

keyDifferences.title: Custom Scan Scripting
keyDifferences.toolA: Not documented as a Checkmarx feature.
keyDifferences.toolB: OpenVAS includes an internal scripting language for writing custom vulnerability tests.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: The ability to write custom detection logic matters for niche or internally developed protocols and systems.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Security engineers who need to write custom network vulnerability tests benefit from OpenVAS's scripting language.

rows.note: Checkmarx Developer Assist and Triage & Remediation Assist
Checkmarx MCP Server
OpenVAS core is $0; Checkmarx requires a custom quote
SOC 2 Type II, ISO 27001, FedRAMP
Via Greenbone's commercial products for OpenVAS

faqs.question: Is Checkmarx free?
faqs.answer: No, Checkmarx One is priced through custom, quote-based sales conversations based on developers, apps, and usage, with no advertised free trial.

faqs.question: Is OpenVAS free?
faqs.answer: Yes, OpenVAS is open source and forms the core of the free Greenbone Community Edition.

faqs.question: Does Checkmarx scan network infrastructure?
faqs.answer: This is not documented as a Checkmarx capability; its documented scope is application source code, dependencies, containers, IaC, and APIs.

faqs.question: Does OpenVAS analyze application source code?
faqs.answer: This is not documented as an OpenVAS capability; its documented scope is authenticated and unauthenticated network protocol scanning.

faqs.question: Which tool uses AI?
faqs.answer: Checkmarx includes AI-powered agents (Developer Assist, Triage & Remediation Assist) and a Checkmarx MCP Server; AI features are not documented for OpenVAS.

faqs.question: Who are these tools built for?
faqs.answer: Checkmarx is built for enterprise application security teams needing broad SDLC coverage with custom pricing, while OpenVAS is built for teams needing a free, self-hosted network vulnerability scanner.

content.verdict: Akeyless and Checkmarx solve different security problems entirely. Akeyless is a SaaS platform for secrets management, machine identity, PKI, and multi-cloud key management, with a published Free plan and an Enterprise custom tier. Checkmarx is an application security testing platform (SAST, DAST, SCA, container, secrets detection, IaC) priced through custom enterprise quotes with no free tier. They're not competitors so much as adjacent pieces of a security stack.
content.bestForToolA: Teams that need to centrally store, rotate, and dynamically deliver secrets, certificates, and encryption keys across multi-cloud environments, including protecting secrets from AI agent context via SecretlessAI.
content.bestForToolB: Enterprise development organizations that need to scan source code, dependencies, containers, and infrastructure-as-code for vulnerabilities across the SDLC, backed by SOC 2 Type II, ISO 27001, and FedRAMP compliance.
content.whoNeedsBoth: A strong complementary scenario: a DevSecOps team uses Checkmarx's Secrets Detection feature to catch hardcoded credentials accidentally committed to source code, while using Akeyless as the actual secrets management platform where those credentials should be stored, rotated, and dynamically delivered instead of hardcoded.

keyDifferences.title: Core Product Category
keyDifferences.toolA: Akeyless centrally stores and dynamically delivers static, dynamic, and rotated secrets to applications and pipelines.
keyDifferences.toolB: Checkmarx statically and dynamically tests application code and infrastructure for security vulnerabilities; it does not store or manage secrets.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: Secrets management and application security testing are different disciplines that both belong in a mature security program but solve different problems.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Platform/infrastructure teams need Akeyless for secret storage; AppSec teams need Checkmarx for vulnerability scanning.

keyDifferences.title: Hardcoded Secrets Detection vs. Management
keyDifferences.toolA: Not documented as scanning source code for hardcoded secrets; Akeyless focuses on storing and rotating secrets that applications retrieve.
keyDifferences.toolB: Checkmarx includes a dedicated Secrets Detection feature that identifies hardcoded secrets and credentials in source code repositories.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: Detecting a leaked secret in code and actually managing that secret's lifecycle are complementary but distinct capabilities.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Teams wanting to both catch leaked secrets (Checkmarx) and properly manage them going forward (Akeyless).

keyDifferences.title: AI-Related Security Features
keyDifferences.toolA: Akeyless offers SecretlessAI, which keeps secrets out of AI agent context while validating agent actions at runtime via Agentic Runtime Authority.
keyDifferences.toolB: Checkmarx offers AI-powered agents (Developer Assist, Triage & Remediation Assist) and a Checkmarx MCP Server to help developers fix vulnerability findings faster.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: Both vendors are investing in AI, but for different purposes — protecting secrets from AI agents versus using AI to accelerate vulnerability remediation.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Teams building AI agent workflows benefit from Akeyless's SecretlessAI; development teams facing large finding backlogs benefit from Checkmarx's AI remediation agents.

keyDifferences.title: Free Tier Availability
keyDifferences.toolA: Akeyless publishes a Free plan with specific quotas: up to 5 clients, 500 static secrets, 5 dynamic secrets, 5 rotated secrets, 3 targets, and 3-day audit log retention.
keyDifferences.toolB: Checkmarx pricing is fully custom and quote-based with no published free tier or advertised free trial.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: A published free tier lets smaller teams start immediately without a sales process, while enterprise platforms often require budget approval upfront.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Small teams and individual developers can start with Akeyless's Free plan immediately; enterprise buyers with dedicated AppSec budgets are the target for Checkmarx.

keyDifferences.title: Compliance Certifications
keyDifferences.toolA: Akeyless holds ISO 27001, PCI-DSS, FIPS 140-3, GDPR, and DORA compliance, among others.
keyDifferences.toolB: Checkmarx holds SOC 2 Type II, ISO 27001 certification, and FedRAMP authorization.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: Both share ISO 27001 but otherwise carry different certifications relevant to different regulatory contexts (payments/crypto vs. government/enterprise).
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Payment and financial services teams may weigh Akeyless's PCI-DSS and FIPS 140-3 certifications; government-adjacent buyers may weigh Checkmarx's FedRAMP authorization.

rows.feature: Hardcoded secrets detection in source code

rows.note: Akeyless SecretlessAI
Developer Assist, Triage & Remediation Assist
Checkmarx MCP Server
Akeyless Free plan: $0, quota-limited

rows.feature: Custom enterprise quote required for top tier

rows.note: Akeyless: ISO 27001, PCI-DSS, FIPS 140-3, GDPR, DORA; Checkmarx: SOC 2 Type II, ISO 27001, FedRAMP

faqs.question: Are Akeyless and Checkmarx direct competitors?
faqs.answer: No, they serve different purposes — Akeyless manages and stores secrets, while Checkmarx scans code and infrastructure for security vulnerabilities, including hardcoded secrets left in source code.

faqs.question: Does Akeyless scan source code for vulnerabilities?
faqs.answer: This is not documented as an Akeyless capability; its documented focus is secrets management, PKI, and multi-cloud key management.

faqs.question: Does Checkmarx manage or store secrets?
faqs.answer: No, Checkmarx's Secrets Detection feature identifies hardcoded secrets in source code, but it does not store, rotate, or dynamically deliver secrets the way Akeyless does.

faqs.question: Is there a free way to try Akeyless?
faqs.answer: Yes, Akeyless offers a Free plan with quotas including up to 5 clients, 500 static secrets, and 3-day audit log retention; Checkmarx does not advertise a free trial.

faqs.question: Do both platforms use AI?
faqs.answer: Yes, but differently — Akeyless's SecretlessAI protects secrets from AI agent context, while Checkmarx's AI agents (Developer Assist, Triage & Remediation Assist) help developers fix security findings.

faqs.question: Which compliance certifications do they share?
faqs.answer: Both Akeyless and Checkmarx hold ISO 27001 certification; Akeyless additionally documents PCI-DSS, FIPS 140-3, GDPR, and DORA, while Checkmarx additionally documents SOC 2 Type II and FedRAMP authorization.

content.verdict: Both are secrets management platforms, but they start from different deployment philosophies. Akeyless is SaaS-first, using patented Distributed Fragments Cryptography and offering a quota-limited Free plan alongside a custom Enterprise tier. HashiCorp Vault is open-source-first, with a fully free, self-hostable Community edition, an Enterprise tier requiring a paid license, and HCP Vault Dedicated as a separate cloud-hosted managed option with a free trial. The choice often comes down to whether a team wants to self-host or consume secrets management as a service.
content.bestForToolA: Teams that want a managed SaaS secrets platform out of the box, need built-in multi-cloud KMS and privileged access management (PAM), or are building AI agent workflows and want SecretlessAI to keep secrets out of agent context.
content.bestForToolB: Teams that want a free, fully self-hostable open-source secrets platform (Vault Community), with the option to later use HCP Vault Dedicated's managed cloud offering (free trial available) or Vault Enterprise for features like the Transform Secrets Engine.
content.whoNeedsBoth: These largely compete for the same job as primary secrets stores, so most teams pick one rather than running both in parallel; the main joint scenario is a multi-cloud or vendor-diversification strategy where different business units standardize on different vaults during a merger or migration, rather than a genuine complementary integration.

keyDifferences.title: Deployment Philosophy
keyDifferences.toolA: Akeyless is a SaaS platform by design, using patented Distributed Fragments Cryptography that fragments cryptographic keys across regions.
keyDifferences.toolB: HashiCorp Vault's Community edition is free, open-source, and self-hosted, with HCP Vault Dedicated available separately as a managed cloud option offering a free trial.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: Self-hosting versus SaaS affects operational overhead, data residency control, and how quickly a team can get started.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Teams wanting to avoid infrastructure management benefit from Akeyless's SaaS-first model; teams wanting full control or needing to self-host for compliance reasons benefit from Vault Community.

keyDifferences.title: Free Tier Structure
keyDifferences.toolA: Akeyless's Free plan has documented quotas: up to 5 clients, 500 static secrets, 5 dynamic secrets, 5 rotated secrets, 3 targets, and 3-day audit log retention.
keyDifferences.toolB: Vault Community is free and open source without documented client or secret count caps, since it runs on infrastructure the user controls, though it lacks the Enterprise-only Transform Secrets Engine (tokenization).
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: Quota-limited SaaS free tiers cap usage explicitly, while self-hosted free tiers are typically limited by infrastructure and missing enterprise-only features instead.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Small teams testing with low secret volumes fit Akeyless's Free plan; teams needing scale without vendor quota limits fit self-hosted Vault Community.

keyDifferences.title: Cryptographic Architecture
keyDifferences.toolA: Akeyless uses patented Distributed Fragments Cryptography, fragmenting keys across regions so no single location holds a complete key.
keyDifferences.toolB: Vault provides encryption as a service through its Transit Secrets Engine but does not document a fragmented-key architecture.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: Fragmented key architectures can reduce single-point-of-compromise risk for the cryptographic material itself.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Security architects evaluating key-compromise resilience may weigh Akeyless's fragmentation approach specifically.

keyDifferences.title: AI Agent Secret Protection
keyDifferences.toolA: Akeyless offers SecretlessAI, a dedicated feature that keeps secrets out of AI agent context while validating agent actions at runtime via Agentic Runtime Authority.
keyDifferences.toolB: Vault documents general identity-based access control for humans, machines, and AI agents, but no dedicated SecretlessAI-equivalent product feature.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: As AI agents increasingly need credentialed access, a purpose-built control layer reduces the risk of secrets leaking into model context.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Teams deploying autonomous AI agents that need runtime-validated, context-free credential access benefit from Akeyless's SecretlessAI.

keyDifferences.title: Published Compliance Certifications
keyDifferences.toolA: Akeyless documents ISO 27001, PCI-DSS, FIPS 140-3, GDPR, DORA, AICPA, and CSA STAR Level One certifications.
keyDifferences.toolB: Formal product compliance certifications are not documented in Vault's public facts; Vault instead offers personnel certification programs (Vault Associate, Vault Operations Professional).
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: Regulated buyers often need documented product-level compliance attestations during procurement, distinct from staff training certifications.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Compliance-driven buyers evaluating documented certifications directly benefit from Akeyless's published list.

Akeyless core is SaaS; HCP Vault Dedicated is Vault's managed cloud option

faqs.question: Is HashiCorp Vault free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Vault Community edition is free and open source; Vault Enterprise requires a valid paid license, and HCP Vault Dedicated offers a free trial.

faqs.question: Is Akeyless free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Akeyless offers a Free plan, though with limited quotas such as up to 5 clients, 500 static secrets, and 3-day audit log retention.

faqs.question: Which one has a free self-hosted option?
faqs.answer: HashiCorp Vault's Community edition is free and open source for self-hosting; Akeyless's Free plan is SaaS-based rather than self-hosted, per the documented facts.

faqs.question: Do both support dynamic secrets?
faqs.answer: Yes, Akeyless's Free plan includes dynamic secrets (up to 5), and Vault has a dedicated Database Secrets Engine that generates short-lived database credentials dynamically.

faqs.question: Which has more documented compliance certifications?
faqs.answer: Akeyless documents a longer list, including ISO 27001, PCI-DSS, FIPS 140-3, GDPR, and DORA; formal product compliance certifications are not documented for Vault in the available facts.

faqs.question: Do both handle AI agent security?
faqs.answer: Akeyless has a dedicated SecretlessAI feature for keeping secrets out of AI agent context; Vault documents identity-based access control that mentions AI agents generally but without a dedicated equivalent feature.

content.verdict: Despite the similar name, these solve different problems. HashiCorp Vault manages machine and application secrets — API keys, database credentials, encryption keys, and certificates — accessed via UI, CLI, or HTTP API. Vaultwarden is a free, open-source Rust server that implements the Bitwarden Client API, letting official Bitwarden mobile, desktop, and browser apps connect to a self-hosted backend for personal and team password management. One is infrastructure plumbing for developers and services; the other is a consumer-style password manager for humans.
content.bestForToolA: Platform and DevOps teams that need to dynamically generate short-lived database credentials, provide encryption as a service via the Transit Secrets Engine, or manage certificates for applications and services.
content.bestForToolB: Individuals, families, or small teams who want a self-hosted, Bitwarden-compatible password vault with Send for secure file/text sharing, organizations with member roles, and MFA options including FIDO2 WebAuthn and YubiKey.
content.whoNeedsBoth: A realistic pairing: an engineering organization uses HashiCorp Vault to manage application and infrastructure secrets like API keys and database credentials, while its employees separately use Vaultwarden as their personal or team password manager for logging into SaaS tools and internal accounts — different secret types serving humans versus machines.

keyDifferences.title: Primary Purpose
keyDifferences.toolA: Vault secures, stores, and tightly controls access to tokens, passwords, certificates, and encryption keys for applications and infrastructure.
keyDifferences.toolB: Vaultwarden is a self-hosted server implementing the Bitwarden Client API for personal vault management, designed for humans storing and sharing their own passwords.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: Machine/application secrets and human login credentials have different lifecycle, rotation, and access patterns.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: DevOps/platform teams need Vault's application secrets management; end users and IT teams managing employee logins need Vaultwarden.

keyDifferences.title: Client Experience
keyDifferences.toolA: Vault is accessed via UI, CLI, or HTTP API, built for developers and operators integrating secrets into pipelines and applications.
keyDifferences.toolB: Vaultwarden is compatible with official Bitwarden mobile, desktop, and browser clients, giving end users a consumer-familiar password manager interface.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: Non-technical end users need a polished client app, while developers need programmatic API access integrated into automation.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: General employees benefit from Vaultwarden's Bitwarden client compatibility; engineers automating secret retrieval benefit from Vault's API-first design.

keyDifferences.title: Dynamic Secret Generation
keyDifferences.toolA: Vault's Database Secrets Engine dynamically generates short-lived database credentials on demand.
keyDifferences.toolB: Not documented as a Vaultwarden capability; Vaultwarden stores static personal and organizational vault items rather than generating dynamic credentials.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: Short-lived, dynamically generated credentials reduce the blast radius of a leaked secret compared to long-lived static passwords.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Teams securing database access for applications benefit from Vault's dynamic secrets engine.

keyDifferences.title: Multi-Factor Authentication Methods
keyDifferences.toolA: Vault documents policy-driven, identity-based access control for humans, machines, and AI agents, without listing specific consumer MFA methods.
keyDifferences.toolB: Vaultwarden documents specific MFA methods: Authenticator apps, Email, FIDO2 WebAuthn, YubiKey, and Duo.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: Password managers need concrete, user-facing MFA options that everyday employees can set up on their own accounts.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Security-conscious individuals and IT admins configuring employee vault security benefit from Vaultwarden's documented MFA method list.

keyDifferences.title: Official Support and Enterprise Tier
keyDifferences.toolA: Vault Community is HashiCorp's official open-source project, with Vault Enterprise available under a paid license and Vault Associate/Operations Professional certification programs.
keyDifferences.toolB: Vaultwarden is an unofficial, community-built project not affiliated with or officially supported by Bitwarden Inc., with no documented enterprise support tier.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: Organizations with compliance or vendor-support requirements need to know whether official commercial support exists.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Enterprises needing vendor-backed support and certification paths benefit from Vault's official Enterprise tier; hobbyists and small teams comfortable with community support benefit from Vaultwarden.

faqs.question: Is Vaultwarden the same as HashiCorp Vault?
faqs.answer: No, despite the similar name, they are unrelated products — Vault manages machine and application secrets, while Vaultwarden is a self-hosted, Bitwarden-compatible personal password manager.

faqs.question: Is Vaultwarden official Bitwarden software?
faqs.answer: No, Vaultwarden is an unofficial, community-built alternative server implementation of the Bitwarden Client API, compatible with official Bitwarden clients but not affiliated with Bitwarden Inc.

faqs.question: Can I use Vault to store my personal passwords?
faqs.answer: Vault is documented as securing tokens, passwords, certificates, and encryption keys for applications and infrastructure via UI, CLI, or API; it is not documented as offering a consumer password-manager client experience the way Vaultwarden does.

faqs.question: Does HashiCorp Vault have an official managed cloud option?
faqs.answer: Yes, HCP Vault Dedicated is referenced as offering a free trial as a cloud-hosted, managed option; Vaultwarden has no documented managed cloud offering.

faqs.question: Are both free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, Vault Community edition is free and open source, and Vaultwarden is free and open source under the AGPL-3.0 license.

faqs.question: What MFA options does Vaultwarden support?
faqs.answer: Vaultwarden supports Authenticator apps, Email, FIDO2 WebAuthn, YubiKey, and Duo for multi-factor authentication.

content.verdict: FusionAuth is a customer identity and access management (CIAM) platform that handles login, SSO, MFA, and RBAC for the applications a company builds, with a free self-hosted Community Edition and cloud plans starting at $162/month. Vaultwarden is a free, open-source, self-hosted password manager server compatible with official Bitwarden apps, used by individuals and teams to store and share their own credentials. They serve adjacent but distinct jobs: authenticating a company's end users versus storing a team's own passwords.
content.bestForToolA: Companies that need to add login, SSO, social authentication, passkeys, or machine-to-machine authentication to their own applications, with SDKs across languages like .NET Core, React, Java, Python, and PHP.
content.bestForToolB: Individuals, families, or small teams who want a free, self-hosted, Bitwarden-compatible password vault for their own personal or shared organizational credentials.
content.whoNeedsBoth: A common real-world pairing: a company uses FusionAuth to power login, SSO, and MFA for its own customer-facing product, while its internal team separately uses Vaultwarden to store and share internal credentials such as admin panel logins and infrastructure passwords that aren't managed through the application's own auth system.

keyDifferences.title: Primary Purpose
keyDifferences.toolA: FusionAuth is a customer identity and access management platform for authenticating and authorizing an application's end users.
keyDifferences.toolB: Vaultwarden is a personal/team password vault for storing and sharing login credentials people already have, not for authenticating into a company's own application.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: Building login for your product and storing your team's passwords are different problems requiring different tools.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Product teams building customer-facing login need FusionAuth; internal IT and small teams managing shared credentials need Vaultwarden.

keyDifferences.title: Pricing Structure
keyDifferences.toolA: FusionAuth's Community Edition is free and self-hosted; cloud plans start at $162/month (Starter, billed annually), rising to Essentials and Enterprise plans starting at $2,970/month.
keyDifferences.toolB: Vaultwarden has no published pricing plans in its documented facts — it is free, open-source software intended for self-hosting.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: Teams scaling into paid CIAM tiers need to budget for FusionAuth's cloud pricing curve, while Vaultwarden's cost is mainly self-hosting infrastructure.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Growing SaaS companies need to plan for FusionAuth's tiered cloud pricing; individuals and small teams benefit from Vaultwarden's fully free model.

keyDifferences.title: Authentication Features for Applications
keyDifferences.toolA: FusionAuth provides SSO, social logins, passwordless magic links, passkeys, and machine-to-machine authentication specifically for securing applications.
keyDifferences.toolB: Vaultwarden documents MFA methods (FIDO2 WebAuthn, YubiKey, Duo) to protect the vault itself, not to authenticate users into third-party applications.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: Securing your own product's login flow requires a dedicated CIAM feature set that a password vault doesn't provide.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Developers building customer-facing authentication flows need FusionAuth's SSO and passwordless features.

keyDifferences.title: Developer SDK Ecosystem
keyDifferences.toolA: FusionAuth is API-first with webhooks and provides SDKs and quickstarts across .NET Core, Angular, Python, iOS, React, Java, Ruby, JavaScript, Vue, Flutter, Go, and PHP.
keyDifferences.toolB: Vaultwarden's integration surface is Bitwarden Client API compatibility for existing official Bitwarden apps, not an SDK ecosystem for embedding auth into other applications.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: Teams integrating authentication into their own codebase need broad language and framework SDK support.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Development teams building custom apps across multiple languages benefit from FusionAuth's SDK breadth.

keyDifferences.title: Support Tiers
keyDifferences.toolA: FusionAuth documents business-day email support on the Essentials plan and 24/7 phone and email support plus a private Slack channel on the Enterprise plan.
keyDifferences.toolB: Vaultwarden is an unofficial, community-built project with no documented official support tiers.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: Mission-critical authentication systems often need guaranteed vendor support SLAs, unlike a self-hosted community password vault.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Enterprises requiring guaranteed support response times benefit from FusionAuth's Enterprise support tier.

rows.note: Vaultwarden's Organizations feature governs vault sharing roles, not app authorization

rows.feature: MFA to secure the tool itself
rows.note: FusionAuth: MFA and passkeys; Vaultwarden: Authenticator, FIDO2 WebAuthn, YubiKey, Duo
FusionAuth Community Edition; Vaultwarden AGPL-3.0
FusionAuth: $162/mo up to $2,970/mo+

faqs.question: Are FusionAuth and Vaultwarden competitors?
faqs.answer: No, they serve different purposes — FusionAuth handles login and SSO for a company's own application, while Vaultwarden is a personal or team password manager for storing existing credentials.

faqs.question: Is FusionAuth free?
faqs.answer: Yes, the Community Edition is free to self-host with core authentication features; paid cloud plans start at $162/month (Starter).

faqs.question: Is Vaultwarden free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Vaultwarden is free, open-source software licensed under AGPL-3.0, with no published pricing plans.

faqs.question: Which tool should I use to add login to my app?
faqs.answer: FusionAuth is built for that — it provides SSO, social logins, magic links, passkeys, and machine-to-machine authentication with SDKs across many languages; Vaultwarden does not provide application login capabilities.

faqs.question: Which tool should my team use to store shared passwords?
faqs.answer: Vaultwarden is built for that — it's a self-hosted, Bitwarden-compatible vault with Organizations, Send, and emergency access features; FusionAuth does not provide personal credential storage.

faqs.question: How does pricing compare at scale?
faqs.answer: FusionAuth's cloud pricing scales from $162/month (Starter) up to Essentials and Enterprise plans starting at $2,970/month, while Vaultwarden has no published pricing since it is free, self-hosted software.
